old_claim.note: Contract Law provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Ancient law-code traditions and legal concepts supplied a shared legal context for written commercial obligations, but the node is not claiming a direct descent from one specific code.

source_shape.source_locator: Chapter 2 links Bronze Age law-code traditions to commercial contracts, notes written-contract requirements for common commercial activities, and describes legal concepts of partnership and written contracts in Old Assyrian trade networks.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source reviews the relationship among Bronze Age law codes, commercial contracts, partnership concepts, and written obligations in Mesopotamian and Old Assyrian trade.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports keeping an ancient legal-code context as a common dependency while removing the later broad Classical contract_law node from the Old Assyrian partnership-contract scope.

ontology_before: Merchant Partnership Contracts depended on a broad Classical contract_law node, making a source-backed Old Assyrian contract form appear later and more generic than the evidence supports.
ontology_after: Merchant Partnership Contracts is scoped to Bronze Age written merchant contracts and keeps codified_law only as a shared ancient legal context, not as a direct claim about one specific law code.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: merchant_partnership_contracts now depends on codified_law as a common dependency and no longer depends on contract_law.
would_reject_if: The node is broadened back to all Classical or global contract law rather than Old Assyrian and Mesopotamian merchant partnership contracts.
A source shows the Old Assyrian partnership-contract evidence requires the later local contract_law node as a direct prerequisite.
The codified_law node is narrowed away from ancient law-code traditions and legal-decision inscriptions.
short_reason: Old Assyrian partnership contracts need ancient legal context, not a later broad Classical contract-law prerequisite.
